text: Epidemiyoloji, hastalıkların dağılımını, modellerini ve belirleyicilerini belirli bir popülasyonda inceleyerek hastalıkları önlemeyi amaçlar.
validation_reason: The original chunk states: "Epidemiology is the study and analysis of the distribution (who, when, and where), patterns and determinants of health and disease conditions in a defined population, and application of this knowledge to prevent diseases." text: John Snow, 19. yüzyıldaki kolera salgınlarını araştırmasıyla modern epidemiyolojinin babası olarak kabul edilir ve çalışmaları halk sağlığı politikalarını şekillendiren kurucu bir olay olarak görülür.

text: Vaka-kontrol çalışmaları, hastalık durumlarına göre denekleri seçen geriye dönük çalışmalardır; hastalık pozitif bir grup (vaka) ile hastalık negatif bir grup (kontrol) karşılaştırılır ve geçmişteki potansiyel maruziyetler incelenir.
validation_reason: The nugget accurately reflects the information in the chunk: 'Case-control studies select subjects based on their disease status. It is a retrospective study. A group of individuals that are disease positive (the "case" group) is compared with a group of disease negative individuals (the "control" group). ... The case-control study looks back through time at potential exposures that both groups (cases and controls) may have encountered.'
storage_reason: This nugget explains a fundamental epidemiological study design (case-control studies), which is important for understanding research methods in the field. It is atomic and well-supported.

text: Epidemiyolojinin daha derin bir anlayışı, maruziyetler ile sağlık sonuçları arasındaki nedensel ilişkileri keşfetmektir, sadece istatistiksel araçlarla ilişkileri açıklamak değil.
validation_reason: The original chunk states: "a deeper understanding of this science is that of discovering causal relationships." This directly supports the nugget's claim about the core purpose of epidemiology.
storage_reason: This nugget clarifies a core objective of epidemiology: discovering causal relationships, which is a crucial distinction. It is atomic and well-supported.

text: Epidemiyoloji, popülasyonlardaki hastalık insidansı ve genel nedensellik ile ilgilenir; bireysel bir hastalığın belirli nedenini (spesifik nedensellik) ele almaz.
validation_reason: The nugget is directly supported by the sentences: 'Epidemiology is concerned with the incidence of disease in populations and does not address the question of the cause of an individual's disease. This question, sometimes referred to as specific causation, is beyond the domain of the science of epidemiology. Epidemiology has its limits at the point where an inference is made that the relationship between an agent and a disease is causal (general causation) and where the magnitude of excess risk attributed to the agent has been determined; that is, epidemiology addresses whether an agent can cause disease, not whether an agent did cause a specific plaintiff's disease.'
storage_reason: This nugget precisely defines the scope of epidemiology, distinguishing between general and specific causation, which is a key conceptual point. It is atomic and well-supported.

text: Epidemiyolojinin amacı, bir ajan ile hastalık arasındaki ilişkinin nedensel olup olmadığına dair çıkarım yapıldığı noktaya kadar uzanır (genel nedensellik).
validation_reason: The nugget is supported by the sentence: 'Epidemiology has its limits at the point where an inference is made that the relationship between an agent and a disease is causal (general causation)'. This implies that understanding causal relationships is a key aspect of epidemiology.
storage_reason: This nugget focuses on a specific aspect of epidemiological aims (inferring general causality). While supported, it's a more detailed point that can be compressed compared to the core definition. It's distinct enough to keep but not as central as the main definition.

text: Sistematik hata veya yanlılık, örnekleme değişkenliği dışındaki herhangi bir nedenden dolayı popülasyondaki gerçek değer ile çalışmada gözlemlenen değer arasında bir fark olduğunda ortaya çıkar.

text: Bir çalışmanın geçerliliği, sistematik hata derecesine bağlıdır ve iç geçerlilik (ölçüm hatalarının olmaması) ile dış geçerlilik (bulguların genel popülasyona genellenebilirliği) olmak üzere iki ana bileşene ayrılır; iç geçerlilik, dış geçerliliğin ön koşuludur.
validation_reason: The nugget accurately states that study validity depends on systematic error, is divided into internal (lack of measurement errors) and external (generalizability to population) validity, and that internal validity is a prerequisite for external validity. This is directly supported by the text: 'The validity of a study is dependent on the degree of systematic error. Validity is usually separated into two components: Internal validity ... implies a lack of error in measurement ... External validity pertains to the process of generalizing the findings of the study to the population ... Internal validity is clearly a prerequisite for external validity'.
storage_reason: This nugget explains the concept of study validity and its components (internal and external validity), which is crucial for evaluating research. It is atomic and well-supported.

text: Karıştırıcılık (confounding), ilgilenilen ana etki(ler) ile karıştırıcılar olarak adlandırılan dışsal faktörlerin etkilerinin bir arada bulunmasından veya karışmasından kaynaklanan bir yanlılıktır.
validation_reason: The nugget provides a direct and accurate translation of the definition of confounding from the text: 'Confounding has traditionally been defined as bias arising from the co-occurrence or mixing of effects of extraneous factors, referred to as confounders, with the main effect(s) of interest'.

text: John Graunt, 1662'de yayımladığı "Natural and Political Observations ... upon the Bills of Mortality" adlı eserinde Londra'daki ölüm kayıtlarını analiz ederek ilk yaşam tablolarından birini sunmuş ve hastalıklarla ilgili birçok teoriye istatistiksel kanıt sağlamıştır.
validation_reason: The chunk states: 'John Graunt, a haberdasher and amateur statistician, published Natural and Political Observations ... upon the Bills of Mortality in 1662. In it, he analysed the mortality rolls in London before the Great Plague, presented one of the first life tables, and reported time trends for many diseases, new and old. He provided statistical evidence for many theories on disease, and also refuted some widespread ideas on them.'
storage_reason: This nugget introduces John Graunt and his pioneering statistical work on mortality data, which is a significant contribution to the quantitative aspects of epidemiology. It is atomic and well-supported.
